narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 67% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, leading to the rejection. This prediction is based on patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on factors like academic rankings, test scores, and backgrounds that best forecast bar success. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd, which is your lowest academic standing among peers and significantly below the average 5.53; this stood out as the top factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as lower rankings in later years often signal challenges in sustaining performance under pressure. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 26, well below the average 36.70, which further tilted the model toward expecting bar struggles since strong standardized test results are key predictors of legal exam success. On a more positive note, your above-average undergrad GPA worked in your favor as the third most influential factor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing by demonstrating your foundational academic strength. Compared to typical full-time students—who make up over 92% of applicants—your middle family income bracket aligned with the norm, so it didn't sway things much either way. While your Year 1 decile of 1st shows an impressive start, the model weighed the later dip and LSAT more heavily in this case. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background; it's the model highlighting risks based on how similar profiles have fared on the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your later-year performance through targeted study habits or additional coursework might also help rebuild that decile ranking. Many students in your position reapply successfully after addressing these gaps—reach out to admissions advisors for personalized strategies, and keep pursuing your legal ambitions with that strong GPA as your foundation.
